candidates.title: Absorption Has a Downstream Cost: Re-evaluating Feature Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders
candidates.summary: Conduct a systematic downstream causal meta-analysis showing that absorption has a unique negative effect on interpretability utility (sparse probing, RAVEL), independent of reconstruction and L0. Complement this with a critique of the first-letter absorption benchmark (degenerate on some families) and a pilot task-agnostic metric showing domain-dependent absorption patterns.
hypotheses: After controlling for L0 sparsity and reconstruction loss, higher absorption rates correlate negatively with downstream interpretability utility (sparse probing F1, RAVEL Cause/Isolation).
The first-letter absorption benchmark is unrepresentative for many SAE families, showing near-zero variance on some open-model families.
A task-agnostic absorption metric shows domain-dependent patterns that do not correlate strongly (r < 0.4) with the first-letter benchmark.
candidates.pilot_focus: Validate E2 with real SAEBench metrics on Pythia-160M (200+ SAEs). Run official absorption metric on 20-30 GPT-2/Pythia checkpoints to confirm variance patterns. Run task-agnostic pilot on 1 SAE x 3 domains x 10 pairs. Expected total: ~2 hours.
candidates.novelty_claim: First controlled causal analysis treating absorption as a predictor of downstream SAE utility. First systematic evidence that the canonical first-letter benchmark may be unrepresentative for some families. First pilot of a domain-dependent, task-agnostic absorption metric.
candidates.alignment_with_spec: Directly addresses spec goals 1 (quantify absorption prevalence) and 3 (develop reproducible evaluation metrics). Fully training-free.
key_papers: Chanin et al. (2024) — A is for Absorption
Wang et al. (2025) — Does Higher Interpretability Imply Better Utility?
risks: E2 effect attenuates on real SAEBench data, weakening the causal-cost claim.
Official absorption metric integration fails or is too slow.
Task-agnostic metric correlates strongly with first-letter benchmark, falsifying the domain-dependence claim.
mitigations: If E2 attenuates, pivot to metric critique (H2/H3) as the lead contribution.
Fallback to SAEBench's built-in absorption evaluation if sae-spelling fails.
Strong correlation would validate the first-letter benchmark---still a useful result.

candidates.title: Towards a Task-Agnostic Absorption Metric: Automated Hierarchy Discovery for Generalizable SAE Evaluation
candidates.summary: If the causal-cost claim (H1) fails validation on real SAEBench data, pivot to a methods paper focused on developing and validating a task-agnostic absorption metric. Use LLM-based automated hierarchy discovery to identify parent-child feature relationships in arbitrary semantic domains, then apply the Chanin et al. causal ablation framework.
hypotheses: A task-agnostic absorption metric will correlate moderately-to-strongly (r > 0.4) with the original first-letter absorption benchmark while enabling absorption measurement across arbitrary semantic domains.
SAEs with higher scores on the task-agnostic metric will exhibit measurably worse downstream interpretability utility on parent-level features.
candidates.pilot_focus: Run hierarchy discovery and absorption detection on Pythia-160M for one domain (geography: continent -> country) with 5-10 parent-child pairs. Expected duration: 10-15 minutes.
candidates.novelty_claim: No prior work has proposed a fully task-agnostic absorption metric using automated hierarchy discovery.
candidates.alignment_with_spec: Addresses spec goal 3 (develop reproducible evaluation metrics). Fully training-free.
key_papers: Chanin et al. (2024) — A is for Absorption
risks: LLM-generated hierarchies may be noisy or hallucinated.
New metric may not correlate with the first-letter benchmark.
Higher engineering complexity than the front-runner.
mitigations: Start with small, easily validated domains; manually audit a random sample.
If correlation is weak, analyze divergence as a negative result.
Scope pilot tightly to 1 SAE x 1 domain before scaling.

candidates.title: Is Feature Absorption a Training Artifact? A Controlled Baseline Study with Random-Decoder Sparse Autoencoders
candidates.summary: Test whether absorption is a training-dynamics pathology or a geometric inevitability of sparse coding. Compare a trained SAE baseline against a random-decoder SAE (frozen decoder, train only encoder) matched for L0 sparsity.
hypotheses: Randomly initialized, frozen-decoder SAEs matched for sparsity will exhibit absorption rates comparable to fully trained SAEs, indicating absorption is a geometric consequence of sparse dictionary learning on hierarchical data.
candidates.pilot_focus: GPT-2-small, layer 8. One trained SAE vs. one random-decoder SAE trained to matched L0. Expected duration: ~15 minutes GPU time.
candidates.novelty_claim: No prior work has applied the canonical feature absorption metric to random-decoder SAE baselines.
candidates.alignment_with_spec: Explicitly violates the training-free constraint. Dropped unless the constraint is relaxed.
key_papers: Chanin et al. (2024) — A is for Absorption
Korznikov et al. (2026) — Sanity Checks for Sparse Autoencoders
risks: Explicitly violates the project's training-free constraint.
Random-decoder SAE may fail to converge to reasonable MSE.
Result may fall in an ambiguous 50-80% range.
mitigations: Only pursue if training-free constraint is explicitly relaxed.
Use TopK for exact sparsity control; monitor MSE during training.

synthesis_notes.front_runner_selection_reason: Cand_a remains the front-runner but has been strategically reframed. The original 'impossibility triangle' / Pareto narrative was downgraded from primary to supporting contribution because the pilot revealed the first-letter absorption proxy is degenerate on GPT-2 Small (zero for 26/27 checkpoints). The new lead contribution is the downstream causal cost analysis (E2), supported by a metric critique (E2/E3) and a domain-dependent absorption pilot (E3). This reframing was mandated by the result_debate verdict (4/10) and the consensus that E2 provides the strongest signal while E1 is currently too compromised to lead.

perspective_weights.contrarian: Highest weight. Provided the central thesis that absorption is not a fixable bug but an intrinsic tradeoff, and the metric critique that current evaluations are selectively reported on a single objective.
perspective_weights.empiricist: Highest weight. Insisted on rigorous controls, pre-registered thresholds, confounder analysis, and the mandatory use of real SAEBench data. The revised experimental design is much stronger because of this.
perspective_weights.innovator: High weight. Contributed the task-agnostic metric idea and the domain-dependence hypothesis (H3), which became a key supporting experiment.
perspective_weights.pragmatist: High weight. Supplied the engineering reality check and confirmed Pythia-160M as the most reliable open-model anchor with abundant SAEBench data.
perspective_weights.theoretical: Medium weight. Provided learning-theoretic intuition for why absorption increases effective complexity, enriching the causal-cost narrative.
perspective_weights.interdisciplinary: Low-medium weight. The ecological niche-partitioning and explaining-away frameworks enriched conceptual vocabulary but the direct experiments were deprioritized due to training-free constraints and novelty collisions.

dropped_ideas: Original 'impossibility triangle' as lead narrative — downgraded because E1 absorption proxy is degenerate and the Pareto claim is untestable with current data.
Random-decoder baseline (Cand_c) — dropped due to training-free constraint violation.
Interdisciplinary LCA-SAE intervention — dropped due to training-free constraint and partial novelty collision.
evidence_driven_revisions: Pilot e1_full_gpt2 showed degenerate absorption proxy (0.0 for 26/27 checkpoints) and unreliable dead-neuron rates at 2k tokens.

Result debate verdict (4/10) concluded E1 is methodologically compromised, E2 has strong signal but needs real-data validation, and H3 is refuted (weak negative correlation with first-letter benchmark).
Verdict: PROCEED with strategic reframing around E2 (downstream causal cost) and E3 (metric critique), not pivot to backup.

novelty_verification_summary.downstream_causal_cost: No prior work has performed a controlled partial-correlation / regression analysis treating absorption as the specific predictor of downstream utility while controlling for L0, reconstruction, and architecture family.
novelty_verification_summary.first_letter_critique: No prior work has provided systematic evidence that the canonical first-letter benchmark is degenerate or unrepresentative for specific SAE families.
novelty_verification_summary.task_agnostic_metric: No prior work has proposed a fully domain-dependent, task-agnostic absorption metric using automated hierarchy discovery.
